audit_type: Sensitive Data Exposure & PII Handling Audit

vulnerabilities.title: Production API Keys and Credentials Committed to Repository
vulnerabilities.description: The .env.local file containing production secrets is committed to the git repository despite being in .gitignore. This file contains multiple critical secrets that can be used to compromise the entire system.
vulnerabilities.data_type: API Keys, Tokens, Database Credentials, Cryptographic Keys

exposed_secrets.secret: DATABASE_URL (Neon PostgreSQL connection string with password)
exposed_secrets.risk: Full database access to production data
Account takeover, unauthorized API calls, SMS spoofing, email hijacking, Stripe payment manipulation

remediation: Immediately rotate ALL credentials listed above
Remove .env.local from git history using 'git filter-branch' or 'git-filter-repo'
Move all secrets to Vercel Environment Variables (not committed)
Use 'git secret' or similar tool to prevent accidental secret commits
Scan git history for any leaked secrets using 'truffleHog' or 'detect-secrets'
Notify third parties (Twilio, Meta, OpenAI, etc.) of potential compromise

vulnerabilities.title: Admin Password Stored as Plaintext
vulnerabilities.description: ADMIN_PASSWORD is stored in plaintext in environment variables instead of being hashed. While the code supports bcrypt verification, the plaintext password in env vars is a security anti-pattern.
remediation: Generate a bcrypt hash for the password: bcrypt.hash(password, 10)
Replace ADMIN_PASSWORD with the hash only
Update auth.ts to remove plaintext password support if feasible
Consider using magic links or OAuth instead of password auth

vulnerabilities.title: Full Client PII Returned in API Response
vulnerabilities.description: The GET /api/clients endpoint returns full client records including email, phone, and address without any filtering or obfuscation. This violates the principle of least privilege for API responses.
remediation: Return only necessary fields for display (avoid email/phone in list views)
Mask PII: show 'test...@example.com' instead of full email
Implement field-level access control based on user role
Add data minimization: only return fields the frontend actually uses

vulnerabilities.title: Quote Secret Tokens Exposed in URL Query Parameters
vulnerabilities.description: Payment links and public endpoints use secret_token in URL parameters. URLs are logged in browsers, servers, and proxies. Tokens in URLs are vulnerable to leakage via referrer headers, browser history, and server logs.

vulnerable_patterns.risk: Token exposed in browser history, referrer headers, server logs
Token in URL query string is logged by servers, CDNs, proxies

remediation: Use POST requests with tokens in request body instead of URL params
Implement short-lived, single-use tokens instead of long-lived secrets
Add Referrer-Policy: no-referrer header to payment pages
Use authenticated sessions instead of bearer tokens in URLs
Log token access attempts and rotate tokens regularly
Consider passwordless links with email verification

vulnerabilities.title: Error Details Returned in API Response
vulnerabilities.description: Error messages sometimes include raw error objects or exception details that could reveal system internals.
vulnerabilities.example: return NextResponse.json({ error: `Erreur envoi email: ${String(err)}` }, { status: 500 });

risk.information_disclosure: Reveals system architecture, library versions, file paths
risk.debugging_aid: Helps attackers craft exploits by understanding error patterns

remediation: Log full errors server-side (Sentry, structured logging)
Return generic error messages to clients: 'Email sending failed'
Never include exception messages, stack traces, or system details in API responses
Use consistent error response format with generic user-facing messages

vulnerabilities.title: Potential Sensitive Data in Console Logs
vulnerabilities.description: Error logging may capture sensitive variables from catch blocks. While the code shows catch-all error handlers with console.error(err), the actual error objects may contain PII or sensitive configuration.
vulnerabilities.data_type: Client Data, API Keys, System Paths
vulnerable_patterns: console.error('Failed to check active promos for quote:', err)
console.error('auto-description on quote create failed:', err)
remediation: Implement structured logging with field masking (e.g., winston, pino)
Never log full error objects - extract message only
Mask sensitive fields: email → 'email[***]', phone → 'phone[***]'
Remove console logs in production, use logger with sensitivity levels
Audit all console.log/console.error statements for PII

vulnerabilities.title: Client PII Stored Unencrypted in Database
vulnerabilities.description: Client names, emails, phone numbers, and addresses are stored in plaintext in the database without encryption at rest. While PostgreSQL supports field-level encryption, it's not implemented.
vulnerabilities.data_type: PII - Names, Emails, Phone Numbers, Addresses
remediation: Implement field-level encryption using pgcrypto or similar
Use application-level encryption with keys managed by AWS KMS or HashiCorp Vault
Encrypt sensitive fields before storing: email, phone, address
Store encryption keys separately from database
Implement key rotation policy
Consider data minimization: store only necessary PII fields

vulnerabilities.title: JWT Sessions Without Additional Security Controls
vulnerabilities.description: The application uses NextAuth v5 with JWT session strategy. While JWT is signed, there's no indication of additional protections like secure cookies, SameSite flags, or short expiration times.
risks: JWT tokens in localStorage are vulnerable to XSS
No indication of secure cookie settings
remediation: Set secure session cookies: httpOnly=true, secure=true, sameSite=strict
Use short JWT expiration times (15-30 minutes)
Implement refresh token rotation
Add CSRF token to state-changing requests
Configure session callback to limit token size

vulnerabilities.title: Timing-Safe Password Comparison Implementation
vulnerabilities.description: While the code implements timingSafeEqual for plaintext passwords (good!), supporting plaintext passwords at all is risky. The comparison is timing-safe but the storage pattern is weak.
remediation: Deprecate plaintext password auth entirely
Migrate all credentials to bcrypt hashes
Use magic links or OIDC/OAuth instead

vulnerabilities.category: API Key Exposure in Request Headers
vulnerabilities.title: API Key Authentication in Custom Headers
vulnerabilities.description: Internal API endpoints use x-api-key header for authentication. While this is acceptable for internal services, the keys are shared and visible in logs/monitoring.
vulnerabilities.vulnerable_pattern: x-api-key header shared across multiple services
remediation: Use individual API keys per service/client
Implement OAuth 2.0 or mTLS for service-to-service auth
Rotate API keys regularly
Monitor API key usage and disable unused keys
Store keys in secrets manager (AWS Secrets Manager, HashiCorp Vault)

vulnerabilities.title: URL Path Not Validated Before Storage
vulnerabilities.description: The path parameter from tracker.js is sliced to 500 chars but not validated. Could potentially store malicious paths if input validation is bypassed.
vulnerabilities.risk: Only length validation, no pattern validation
remediation: Validate path against whitelist of expected paths
Use URL.parse() to validate structure
Reject paths with suspicious patterns (/admin, /private, etc.)
Implement CSP to prevent script injection via stored path

vulnerabilities.category: Insufficient Input Validation on Sensitive Data
vulnerabilities.title: Phone Number Not Validated Before Storage
vulnerabilities.description: Phone numbers are extracted via regex but not validated against known patterns. Could store invalid or malicious data.
vulnerabilities.risk: Only basic digit extraction, no validation of valid area codes or formats
remediation: Validate against E.164 format for phone numbers
Verify area code is valid for region
Reject obviously invalid patterns (all zeros, sequential, etc.)
Implement phone number verification via SMS or API

key_findings: CRITICAL: Production secrets (.env.local) are committed to git repository
HIGH: API responses expose full client PII without filtering
HIGH: Secret tokens used in URL query parameters (vulnerable to logging)
HIGH: Plaintext admin password stored in environment variables
MEDIUM: Client PII stored unencrypted in PostgreSQL database
MEDIUM: Error messages may expose system details
MEDIUM: Sensitive data may be captured in console logs

steps: 1. Rotate Neon database password and update DATABASE_URL
2. Disable and regenerate all Twilio credentials
4. Rotate all API keys (Anthropic, OpenRouter, Composio, Zapier, Resend, Meta)
6. Delete repository secrets from git history (use git-filter-repo)

immediate_actions_required.action: Move all secrets to secure secret management
steps: 1. Use Vercel Environment Variables for production secrets (not committed)
2. Implement local .env.local with local dev-only credentials
3. Add pre-commit hook to prevent secret commits (detect-secrets)
4. Audit git history for any leaked secrets

immediate_actions_required.action: Implement data minimization in API responses
steps: 1. Audit all API endpoints returning PII
2. Return only necessary fields for frontend
3. Mask sensitive fields in list views

compliance_impact.gdpr: HIGH - Client PII stored unencrypted violates data protection requirements
compliance_impact.pci_dss: CRITICAL - If storing payment card details (check Stripe integration)
compliance_impact.soc2: HIGH - Audit logging and access controls need strengthening

testing_recommendations: Scan current .env files for API keys using 'gitleaks'
Test API responses for PII leakage
Review database for unencrypted sensitive fields
Audit logs for sensitive data exposure
